---
title: "LLM Integration Plan"
subtitle: "CRPD Disability Rights Data Dashboard — Requirements & Development Plan"
author: "Institute on Disability and Public Policy (IDPP), American University"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    number-depth: 3
    theme: cosmo
    highlight-style: github
    code-fold: true
    code-copy: true
    embed-resources: true
    fig-align: center
    callout-appearance: minimal
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    geometry: margin=1in
    fontsize: 11pt
execute:
  echo: true
  eval: false
---

---

# Executive Summary {.unnumbered}

This document defines the full technical requirements and phased development plan for integrating
Large Language Models (LLMs) into the CRPD Disability Rights Data Dashboard. The dashboard
currently serves researchers, policymakers, and disability rights advocates by surfacing patterns
across 585 UN Treaty Body documents from 155 countries (2010–2026) using rule-based keyword
analytics, TF-IDF, and LDA.

LLM integration will elevate the platform from a *data viewer* into a *research assistant* —
enabling natural language queries, document-grounded answers, AI-generated policy briefs, and
semantic search that goes far beyond keyword matching.

The chosen approach is:

> **PDF → Markdown conversion → text chunking → vector embeddings → FAISS index → RAG with Ollama llama3 / Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile**

This pipeline is run **once offline**, producing a lightweight search index that powers all
user-facing LLM features at query time with minimal cost and latency.

::: {.callout-important}
## Critical Finding: Existing `clean_text` is Unsuitable for LLM Use

The current `clean_text` column in `crpd_reports.csv` has had all punctuation removed and
all text lowercased for NLP keyword matching. This makes it unsuitable as LLM input — a
model given unpunctuated, lowercase text produces materially worse responses. The original
PDF source files must be re-processed through a proper conversion pipeline before LLM
integration can proceed.
:::

---

# Project Context

## What the Dashboard Currently Does

The CRPD Disability Rights Data Dashboard is a **Streamlit** application deployed on
Posit Cloud (`idpp.connect.posit.cloud/crpd-dashboard`). It provides:

- An interactive world map of CRPD reporting by country
- Temporal trend analysis across the 2010–2026 reporting cycle
- CRPD article frequency analysis via keyword matching
- Medical model vs. rights-based model language tracking
- Article co-occurrence heatmaps
- TF-IDF-based distinctive terms by document type
- N-gram phrase extraction

## Current Analytics Stack

| Component | Technology | Limitation |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Text matching | Regex + word boundaries | Exact match only; no semantic understanding |
| Article frequency | Keyword counting | Misses synonyms, contextual mentions |
| Topic modeling | LDA (scikit-learn) | No interpretable labels; requires tuning |
| Model classification | Keyword lexicons | Binary; no nuance or context |
| Search | String contains | No semantic similarity |

## What LLM Integration Adds

| New Capability | User Benefit |
|----------------|-------------|
| Natural language Q&A | Ask questions in plain English; get grounded answers |
| Semantic search | Find relevant documents beyond exact keywords |
| Document summarization | Instant summaries of country profiles or filtered subsets |
| Policy brief generation | Downloadable research briefs from any filter state |
| AI-interpreted charts | Plain-English explanation of any visualization |
| Document-grounded RAG | Answers citing specific countries, years, and document types |

## Corpus Statistics

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Total documents | 585 |
| Countries covered | 155 |
| Temporal range | 2010 – 2026 |
| Document language | English (all) |
| Average word count | 11,071 words/document |
| Max word count | 69,735 words |
| Estimated total tokens | ~8.4 million tokens (full corpus) |
| Document types | 5 (State Reports, LOI, Written Replies, Concluding Observations, Responses to COs) |

---

# Architecture Overview

## High-Level System Design

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    OFFLINE (Run Once)                               │
│                                                                     │
│  585 PDFs ──► pymupdf4llm ──► Markdown ──► Chunker ──► Embedder   │
│               (PDF → MD)      files       (~15,000     (sentence-   │
│                                           chunks)      transformers)│
│                                               │                     │
│                                               ▼                     │
│                                     FAISS Index + Metadata JSON    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                               │
                                    committed to repo / Posit Cloud
                                               │
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    ONLINE (Per User Request)                        │
│                                                                     │
│  User Question                                                      │
│       │                                                             │
│       ▼                                                             │
│  Embed question (sentence-transformers, local, free)               │
│       │                                                             │
│       ▼                                                             │
│  FAISS nearest-neighbor search → top 6–8 relevant chunks           │
│       │                                                             │
│       ▼                                                             │
│  Build prompt: [system] + [data context] + [retrieved chunks]      │
│       │                                                             │
│       ▼                                                             │
│  Ollama / Groq API call → streamed response                         │
│       │                                                             │
│       ▼                                                             │
│  Display answer with source citations (country, year, doc type)    │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Repository Structure After Integration

```
crpd-dashboard/
├── app.py
├── requirements.txt               ← updated with new dependencies
├── .streamlit/
│   └── secrets.toml               ← GROQ_API_KEY (git-ignored)
├── data/
│   ├── crpd_reports.csv           ← existing
│   ├── pdfs/                      ← NEW: source PDF files (585 files)
│   ├── markdown/                  ← NEW: converted markdown (585 .md files)
│   ├── embeddings.npy             ← NEW: chunk embeddings (~25 MB)
│   ├── faiss_index.bin            ← NEW: FAISS search index (~20 MB)
│   └── chunks_metadata.json       ← NEW: chunk text + metadata
├── LLM_Development/
│   ├── LLM_Integration_Plan.qmd   ← this document
│   └── build_knowledge_base.py    ← NEW: offline pipeline script
├── scripts/
│   └── (existing scripts)
└── src/
    ├── llm.py                     ← NEW: all LLM/RAG logic
    ├── tab_chat.py                ← NEW: AI Research Assistant standalone page (page == "chat")
    ├── tab_analyze.py             ← unmodified for chat; still hosts Article Coverage, Model Shift, etc.
    ├── tab_overview.py            ← modified: add AI Insights panel
    ├── tab_explore.py             ← modified: add Report Generation (Phase 4)
    └── (existing modules)
```

---

# Step 1 — PDF to Markdown Conversion

## Why This Step is Mandatory

The original UN Treaty Body PDF documents contain rich structure: numbered article headings,
paragraph references, committee recommendations formatted as bullet lists, tables, and proper
sentence casing with punctuation. The current `clean_text` in the CSV has stripped all of this.

Providing an LLM with `"the committee recommends that the state party take all necessary
measures to ensure"` produces significantly better output than the current `"the committee
recommends that the state party take all necessary measures to ensure"` (identical here, but
the real data is fully lowercase and unpunctuated throughout). Structure preservation — article
headings, paragraph numbers, section titles — allows the LLM to reason about *where* in a
document a passage comes from, which matters for citations and accuracy.

## Comparison of PDF-to-Markdown Libraries

### Option 1: `pymupdf4llm` ⭐ Recommended Default

**Package:** `pymupdf4llm`
**Install:** `pip install pymupdf4llm`

**Strengths:**

- Built specifically for LLM input pipelines — output is clean, well-structured Markdown
- Preserves headings, paragraph structure, multi-column layouts, and tables
- Fast: processes a typical CRPD document (~10,000 words) in under 1 second
- Handles native digital PDFs (no OCR needed — UN documents are digital, not scanned)
- Active development and LLM-community adoption
- Minimal configuration required — works out of the box

**Weaknesses:**

- Occasionally struggles with complex multi-column UN letterhead formatting
- Table extraction can be imperfect for heavily merged cells

**Sample usage:**

```{python}
import pymupdf4llm

md_text = pymupdf4llm.to_markdown("Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1.pdf")
# Returns clean Markdown string with headings, paragraphs, tables
```

**Expected output quality for CRPD documents:** Excellent. UN Treaty Body documents use
standard PDF structure with clear heading hierarchies, making them ideal for pymupdf4llm.

---

### Option 2: `docling` (IBM Research) ⭐ Best Quality

**Package:** `docling`
**Install:** `pip install docling`

**Strengths:**

- State-of-the-art document understanding pipeline developed by IBM Research
- Uses AI-based layout analysis — detects headings, figures, tables, lists accurately
- Best-in-class output structure; preserves document hierarchy most faithfully
- Handles both digital PDFs and scanned/OCR documents
- Supports export to Markdown, JSON, and structured DoclingDocument format

**Weaknesses:**

- Significantly slower: 5–15 seconds per document vs. under 1 second for pymupdf4llm
- Heavier installation with additional model downloads on first run (~500 MB)
- Overkill for well-structured digital PDFs like UN Treaty Body documents

**Sample usage:**

```{python}
from docling.document_converter import DocumentConverter

converter = DocumentConverter()
result = converter.convert("Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1.pdf")
md_text = result.document.export_to_markdown()
```

**When to use over pymupdf4llm:** If initial pymupdf4llm output quality is unsatisfactory on
a sample of CRPD documents, switch to docling for the full conversion run.

---

### Option 3: `marker`

**Package:** `marker-pdf`
**Install:** `pip install marker-pdf`

**Strengths:**

- High-quality Markdown output, particularly strong on academic and legal documents
- Built on Surya OCR — handles scanned documents well
- Produces clean, readable Markdown with proper heading nesting

**Weaknesses:**

- GPU recommended for reasonable speed (CPU is slow: 30–60s per document)
- Heavy dependency footprint
- Designed for scanned/academic papers; CRPD documents are native digital PDFs that do
  not require OCR capability

**Verdict:** Not recommended for this corpus. The documents are native digital PDFs.
The OCR capability adds cost without benefit here.

---

### Option 4: `markitdown` (Microsoft)

**Package:** `markitdown`
**Install:** `pip install markitdown`

**Strengths:**

- General-purpose file converter (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, HTML, images)
- Simple API, maintained by Microsoft
- Useful if the team ever needs to process documents in multiple formats

**Weaknesses:**

- PDF-specific quality is lower than pymupdf4llm or docling
- Less control over output formatting

**Verdict:** Good as a secondary utility for format diversity; not the best choice for
PDF-primary pipelines.

---

### Option 5: `pdfplumber` + Custom Markdown

**Package:** `pdfplumber`
**Install:** `pip install pdfplumber`

**Strengths:**

- Very precise control over text extraction at the page, line, and character level
- Excellent table extraction
- Lightweight and fast

**Weaknesses:**

- Requires writing custom logic to reconstruct headings and paragraph structure from
  raw character positions — substantial development effort
- Output quality is only as good as the custom logic written around it

**Verdict:** Not recommended. pymupdf4llm achieves the same result with far less code
and better default output.

---

### PDF-to-Markdown Library Summary Table

| Library | Quality | Speed | OCR | Effort | Recommended Use |
|---------|---------|-------|-----|--------|-----------------|
| **pymupdf4llm** | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★★ | No | Minimal | **Primary choice** |
| **docling** | ★★★★★ | ★★☆☆☆ | Yes | Low | Fallback if quality insufficient |
| marker | ★★★★☆ | ★★☆☆☆ | Yes | Low | Scanned documents only |
| markitdown | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | No | Minimal | Multi-format pipelines |
| pdfplumber | ★★★☆☆ | ★★★★☆ | No | High | Only if full control needed |

---

# Step 2 — Text Chunking

## Why Chunking is Necessary

CRPD documents average 11,071 words (~14,400 tokens). Even with large LLM context windows,
passing an entire document for every query is:

- **Too expensive** — 15,000 tokens × 6 documents retrieved = 84,000 input tokens per query
- **Too noisy** — the LLM receives irrelevant content from unrelated sections
- **Not scalable** — cross-document questions cannot be answered by passing one document at a time

Chunking splits each document into focused segments. The vector search retrieves only the
most relevant 6–8 chunks, giving the LLM a tight, relevant context window.

## Chunking Strategy Options

### Strategy 1: Fixed-Size Chunking with Overlap ⭐ Recommended Starting Point

Split text into fixed word-count windows with overlapping edges to avoid cutting sentences
mid-thought.

```{python}
def chunk_fixed(text: str, chunk_size: int = 500, overlap: int = 75) -> list[str]:
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    start = 0
    while start < len(words):
        end = min(start + chunk_size, len(words))
        chunks.append(" ".join(words[start:end]))
        start += chunk_size - overlap
    return chunks
```

**Parameters for CRPD corpus:**

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| `chunk_size` | 500 words | ~650 tokens; fits well within embedding model limits |
| `overlap` | 75 words | Prevents context loss at chunk boundaries |
| Expected chunks per document | ~25 | 11,071 ÷ (500 - 75) ≈ 26 |
| Total corpus chunks | ~15,000 | 585 docs × ~26 chunks |

---

### Strategy 2: Semantic / Paragraph Chunking ⭐ Better Quality

Instead of fixed word counts, split at natural paragraph and section boundaries in the
converted Markdown. Since pymupdf4llm preserves headings (`#`, `##`, `###`) and paragraph
breaks, these can be used as natural split points.

```{python}
import re

def chunk_by_section(md_text: str, max_words: int = 600) -> list[str]:
    """Split Markdown at heading boundaries, merging small sections."""
    # Split on headings
    sections = re.split(r'\n(?=#{1,3} )', md_text)
    chunks = []
    buffer = ""

    for section in sections:
        combined = buffer + "\n" + section if buffer else section
        if len(combined.split()) <= max_words:
            buffer = combined
        else:
            if buffer:
                chunks.append(buffer.strip())
            # If single section is too long, apply fixed chunking
            if len(section.split()) > max_words:
                chunks.extend(chunk_fixed(section, chunk_size=max_words, overlap=60))
                buffer = ""
            else:
                buffer = section

    if buffer:
        chunks.append(buffer.strip())

    return chunks
```

**Why this is better:** Each chunk contains a coherent, self-contained section of the
document. When retrieved, the LLM receives context that is already thematically unified —
e.g., the full text of a document's section on Article 13, rather than 500 words that
happen to straddle two sections.

---

### Strategy 3: Sentence Window Chunking (Advanced)

Embed at the sentence level, but retrieve a window of surrounding sentences for context.
This maximizes retrieval precision (the embedding represents exactly one sentence) while
providing the LLM with sufficient surrounding context.

This approach is more complex to implement and is not necessary for a 585-document corpus.
It is worth considering if retrieval quality in Strategy 1 or 2 is insufficient.

---

### Chunking Strategy Comparison

| Strategy | Retrieval Precision | Implementation Complexity | Recommended |
|----------|--------------------|-----------------------------|-------------|
| Fixed-size with overlap | Good | Low | Yes — start here |
| Semantic (by section) | Better | Medium | Yes — upgrade to this |
| Sentence window | Best | High | Only if above insufficient |

---

## Metadata Attachment

Every chunk must carry metadata so that retrieved results can be cited properly in responses.

```{python}
chunk_record = {
    "text":        "<the chunk text>",
    "chunk_id":    42,
    "doc_id":      "Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1",
    "country":     "Italy",
    "year":        2013,
    "doc_type":    "state report",
    "region":      "Europe",
    "subregion":   "Western/Eastern/Northern/Southern Europe",
    "symbol":      "CRPD/C/ITA/1",
    "source_file": "Italy_2013_CRPD_C_ITA_1.pdf",
    "chunk_index": 3   # position within document
}
```

This metadata enables:

- Cited answers: *"According to Italy's 2013 State Report…"*
- Filtered retrieval: only search chunks from a specific region or document type
- Source linking: surface the original document reference for the user

---

# Step 3 — Vector Embeddings

## What Embeddings Do

An embedding model converts a chunk of text into a dense numerical vector (e.g., 768
floating-point numbers) that encodes its semantic meaning. Chunks with similar meaning have
vectors that are geometrically close. This enables **semantic search**: a user's question is
embedded into the same space, and the nearest chunks are retrieved regardless of exact word
overlap.

**Example:**

> Query: *"barriers to accessing courts for people with disabilities"*
> Retrieved: chunks containing *"procedural accommodations"*, *"legal aid for disabled persons"*,
> *"access to justice mechanisms"* — even if those exact words do not appear in the query.

This is fundamentally superior to the current regex keyword search.

## Embedding Model Options

All models below run **locally and for free** using the `sentence-transformers` library.
There is no API cost for embeddings.

---

### Option 1: `all-mpnet-base-v2` ⭐ Recommended

**Dimensions:** 768
**Max input tokens:** 384 tokens (~290 words)
**Speed:** ~2,000 chunks/minute on CPU
**Install:** Included in `sentence-transformers`

This is the highest-quality general-purpose English embedding model in the
sentence-transformers library. It was trained on a large diverse corpus and consistently
scores at the top of the MTEB (Massive Text Embedding Benchmark) for semantic search tasks.
Ideal for CRPD documents which are formal English legal/policy text.

```{python}
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
model = SentenceTransformer("all-mpnet-base-v2")
embeddings = model.encode(texts, batch_size=64, show_progress_bar=True)
```

---

### Option 2: `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` ⭐ Strong Alternative

**Dimensions:** 768
**Max input tokens:** 512 tokens (~380 words)
**Speed:** ~2,000 chunks/minute on CPU

Specifically fine-tuned for **question-answering retrieval** — the exact use case here.
Trained on 215M question-answer pairs. Outperforms `all-mpnet-base-v2` on asymmetric
search tasks (short question → long document passage).

**When to choose this over `all-mpnet-base-v2`:** If RAG Q&A quality is the primary
concern (which it is), this model may give measurably better retrieval results.
Worth running a side-by-side evaluation on 20–30 sample queries.

```{python}
model = SentenceTransformer("multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1")
```

---

### Option 3: `all-MiniLM-L6-v2` — Fastest Option

**Dimensions:** 384
**Max input tokens:** 256 tokens (~190 words)
**Speed:** ~15,000 chunks/minute on CPU

6× faster and half the storage of mpnet models. Quality is lower but still good.
Use this if Posit Cloud CPU constraints make embedding time prohibitive, or for
rapid prototyping before switching to a higher-quality model.

```{python}
model = SentenceTransformer("all-MiniLM-L6-v2")
```

---

### Option 4: OpenAI `text-embedding-3-small`

**Dimensions:** 1,536
**API Cost:** ~$0.02 per million tokens (~$0.15 for full corpus)
**Quality:** Excellent

High-quality commercial embeddings. The one-time cost for the full corpus is negligible
(under $0.20). However, this adds an OpenAI dependency and API key requirement.
The free local models are comparable in quality for this use case.

**Verdict:** Not recommended. The sentence-transformers local models are free and
perform comparably on English legal text.

---

### Option 5: Cohere `embed-english-v3.0`

**Dimensions:** 1,024
**API Cost:** Free tier available; ~$0.10 per million tokens
**Quality:** Excellent, particularly strong on retrieval tasks

A strong commercial alternative with an embeddings-specific API. Similar verdict to
OpenAI: good quality, but the free local option is sufficient.

---

### Embedding Model Summary Table

| Model | Dims | Quality | Speed (CPU) | Cost | Recommended |
|-------|------|---------|-------------|------|-------------|
| **all-mpnet-base-v2** | 768 | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | Free | **Yes — default** |
| **multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1** | 768 | ★★★★★ | ★★★☆☆ | Free | **Yes — Q&A focus** |
| all-MiniLM-L6-v2 | 384 | ★★★★☆ | ★★★★★ | Free | Prototyping only |
| text-embedding-3-small | 1,536 | ★★★★★ | N/A (API) | ~$0.15 corpus | Optional |
| cohere embed-english-v3 | 1,024 | ★★★★★ | N/A (API) | Free tier | Optional |

---

# Step 4 — Vector Store

## Purpose

The vector store indexes all chunk embeddings and enables fast approximate nearest-neighbor
search. When a user submits a question, the question is embedded and the store returns the
top-k most similar chunk vectors in milliseconds.

## Options

### Option 1: FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) ⭐ Recommended

**Package:** `faiss-cpu`
**Install:** `pip install faiss-cpu`

**Why this is right for this project:**

- **No server required** — FAISS is an in-process library. The index is a file on disk.
  Load it once at app startup; keep it in memory.
- **Fast** — Searches ~15,000 chunks in under 5 milliseconds on CPU
- **Zero operational overhead** — No Docker, no external service, no network latency
- **Works perfectly on Posit Cloud** — just a binary file committed to the repo

**Index type for this corpus:**

Use `IndexFlatIP` (Inner Product, i.e., cosine similarity after L2 normalization) for
exact nearest-neighbor search. With 15,000 chunks, approximate search (IVF, HNSW) gives
no meaningful speed benefit and adds complexity. Exact search is fine at this scale.

```{python}
import faiss
import numpy as np

# Build index
dim = embeddings.shape[1]  # 768
faiss.normalize_L2(embeddings)
index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
index.add(embeddings)
faiss.write_index(index, "data/faiss_index.bin")

# Search at query time
query_vec = model.encode(["your question here"])
faiss.normalize_L2(query_vec)
scores, indices = index.search(query_vec, k=8)  # top 8 chunks
```

---

### Option 2: ChromaDB

**Package:** `chromadb`
**Install:** `pip install chromadb`

**Strengths:**

- Designed specifically for LLM/RAG applications
- Built-in metadata filtering (e.g., retrieve only chunks from a specific region or year)
- Persistent storage with a simple Python API
- Active development and large community

**Weaknesses:**

- Heavier than FAISS — adds a local SQLite dependency
- Slight overhead vs. FAISS for in-memory search at this scale
- More configuration needed for Posit Cloud deployment

**When to use over FAISS:** If metadata-filtered retrieval becomes important (e.g.,
*"only search within African country documents"*), Chroma's built-in filtering is cleaner
than post-filtering FAISS results manually. At 15,000 chunks, however, FAISS with
post-filter is fast enough.

```{python}
import chromadb

client = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="data/chroma_db")
collection = client.get_or_create_collection("crpd_chunks")

# Add chunks
collection.add(
    documents=[c["text"] for c in chunks],
    metadatas=[{k: v for k, v in c.items() if k != "text"} for c in chunks],
    ids=[str(c["chunk_id"]) for c in chunks]
)

# Query
results = collection.query(query_texts=["your question"], n_results=8)
```

---

### Option 3: LanceDB

**Package:** `lancedb`
**Install:** `pip install lancedb`

**Strengths:**

- Columnar storage format — enables filtering on metadata columns efficiently
- Fast for both vector search and SQL-style filters simultaneously
- Embedded (no server), modern design

**Weaknesses:**

- Less community adoption than FAISS or Chroma
- More overhead for a corpus this small

**Verdict:** Worth considering if the project scales to tens of thousands of documents.
Overkill for 15,000 chunks.

---

### Option 4: Pinecone / Qdrant / Weaviate (Hosted Vector Databases)

Managed cloud vector databases with REST APIs. Production-grade for large corpora (millions
of vectors) and multi-tenant applications.

**Verdict:** Not recommended for this project. The corpus is small enough that a
file-based solution (FAISS or Chroma) is simpler, faster, cheaper, and has no
infrastructure dependency.

---

### Vector Store Summary Table

| Store | Type | Metadata Filter | Complexity | Recommended |
|-------|------|----------------|------------|-------------|
| **FAISS** | In-process | Post-filter | Low | **Yes — default** |
| **ChromaDB** | In-process | Built-in | Medium | Yes — if filtering needed |
| LanceDB | In-process | Built-in | Medium | Scaling only |
| Pinecone | Cloud API | Built-in | Low (API) | Not needed |
| Qdrant | Self-hosted | Built-in | High | Not needed |
| Weaviate | Self-hosted | Built-in | High | Not needed |

---

# Step 5 — LLM Selection

## The LLM's Role in This System

The LLM is the final stage of the RAG pipeline. It receives:

1. A **system prompt** defining its role and rules
2. A **data context** block summarizing the current filtered state of the dashboard
3. **Retrieved document chunks** from the FAISS search
4. The **user's question**

It does not need to memorize the corpus — it only needs to reason over the chunks
provided per query. This means model size and training data are less important than
**instruction-following quality** and **reasoning over legal/policy text**.

## Recommended LLMs: Ollama llama3 (Local) + Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (Groq API)

**Why this combination is the right choice for CRPD documents:**

- **Ollama llama3** runs locally for free — ideal for high-volume, simple tasks (summaries, auto-insights)
- **Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile** (Groq API) provides strong performance on formal, structured policy and legal text
- Excellent instruction-following — system prompt rules are reliably obeyed
- 128K context window on Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile — can handle more retrieved
  chunks per query without degradation
- Streaming support for responsive UI
- Cost-effective: local inference for simple tasks, free-tier API for complex ones (Groq free tier: 30 req/min, 14,400 req/day)

### Model Tiers

| Model | Context | Quality | Speed | Cost (per M tokens) | Use For |
|-------|---------|---------|-------|---------------------|---------|
| `llama3` (Ollama, local) | 8K | ★★★☆☆ | Very fast | Free (local) | Simple summaries, auto-insights |
| `llama-3.3-70b-versatile` (Groq) | 128K | ★★★★☆ | Very fast | Free (30 req/min, 14,400 req/day) | Chat Q&A, RAG, report generation |

**Recommended routing strategy:**

```
Simple insight panel (Phase 1)     →  Ollama llama3        (free, local)
Chat Q&A (Phase 2)                 →  Groq                 (free tier)
RAG deep Q&A (Phase 3)             →  Groq                 (free tier)
Report generation (Phase 4)        →  Groq                 (free tier)
```

---

# Step 6 — Full Offline Build Pipeline

## Prerequisites

Before running the build script:

1. **Obtain the 585 source PDF files** from the UN Treaty Body Database or from whoever
   originally extracted the `clean_text` column. Place them in `data/pdfs/`. File names
   match the `file_name` column in `crpd_reports.csv`.

2. **Install dependencies:**

```bash
pip install pymupdf4llm sentence-transformers faiss-cpu groq ollama
```

3. **Set the API key** in `.streamlit/secrets.toml`:

```toml
GROQ_API_KEY = "gsk_..."
```

## Build Script: `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py`

```{python}
"""
CRPD Knowledge Base Builder
============================
Converts 585 CRPD PDFs into a searchable vector knowledge base.

Run once:
    python LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py

Outputs:
    data/markdown/          — Converted Markdown files (one per PDF)
    data/embeddings.npy     — Chunk embeddings matrix
    data/faiss_index.bin    — FAISS search index
    data/chunks_metadata.json — Chunk text and metadata

Estimated runtime: ~15–20 minutes on CPU (mostly embedding time)
"""

import json
import re
import numpy as np
import faiss
import pandas as pd
import pymupdf4llm
from pathlib import Path
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

# ── Configuration ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PDF_DIR       = Path("data/pdfs")
MARKDOWN_DIR  = Path("data/markdown")
OUTPUT_DIR    = Path("data")

EMBEDDING_MODEL = "all-mpnet-base-v2"   # or "multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1"
CHUNK_SIZE      = 500   # words per chunk
CHUNK_OVERLAP   = 75    # overlapping words at chunk boundaries
BATCH_SIZE      = 64    # embedding batch size

# ── Helpers ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def chunk_by_section(md_text: str, max_words: int = CHUNK_SIZE,
                     overlap: int = CHUNK_OVERLAP) -> list[str]:
    """Split Markdown at heading boundaries; fall back to fixed chunking."""
    sections = re.split(r'\n(?=#{1,3} )', md_text)
    chunks, buffer = [], ""
    for section in sections:
        combined = (buffer + "\n" + section).strip() if buffer else section.strip()
        if len(combined.split()) <= max_words:
            buffer = combined
        else:
            if buffer:
                chunks.append(buffer)
            if len(section.split()) > max_words:
                # Fixed-size fallback for very long sections
                words = section.split()
                for i in range(0, len(words), max_words - overlap):
                    chunks.append(" ".join(words[i:i + max_words]))
                buffer = ""
            else:
                buffer = section.strip()
    if buffer:
        chunks.append(buffer)
    return [c for c in chunks if len(c.split()) >= 20]  # discard tiny fragments


# ── Step 1: PDF → Markdown ──────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 60)
print("STEP 1: Converting PDFs to Markdown")
print("=" * 60)
MARKDOWN_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
df = pd.read_csv("data/crpd_reports.csv")

converted, missing, skipped = 0, 0, 0
for _, row in df.iterrows():
    pdf_path = PDF_DIR / row["file_name"]
    md_path  = MARKDOWN_DIR / row["file_name"].replace(".pdf", ".md")

    if md_path.exists():
        skipped += 1
        continue
    if not pdf_path.exists():
        print(f"  [MISSING] {row['file_name']}")
        missing += 1
        continue

    md_text = pymupdf4llm.to_markdown(str(pdf_path))
    md_path.write_text(md_text, encoding="utf-8")
    converted += 1
    if converted % 50 == 0:
        print(f"  Converted {converted} files...")

print(f"\n  Done: {converted} converted, {skipped} skipped, {missing} missing PDFs\n")


# ── Step 2: Markdown → Chunks ───────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 60)
print("STEP 2: Chunking documents")
print("=" * 60)

all_chunks = []
chunk_id = 0

for _, row in df.iterrows():
    md_path = MARKDOWN_DIR / row["file_name"].replace(".pdf", ".md")
    if not md_path.exists():
        continue

    text = md_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
    chunks = chunk_by_section(text, max_words=CHUNK_SIZE, overlap=CHUNK_OVERLAP)

    for idx, chunk_text in enumerate(chunks):
        all_chunks.append({
            "chunk_id":    chunk_id,
            "text":        chunk_text,
            "doc_id":      row["file_name"].replace(".pdf", ""),
            "country":     str(row["country"]),
            "year":        int(row["year"]) if pd.notna(row["year"]) else None,
            "doc_type":    str(row["doc_type"]),
            "region":      str(row["region"]),
            "subregion":   str(row.get("subregion", "")),
            "symbol":      str(row.get("symbol", "")),
            "source_file": str(row["file_name"]),
            "chunk_index": idx,
        })
        chunk_id += 1

print(f"  Total chunks: {len(all_chunks)}")
print(f"  Avg chunks per document: {len(all_chunks) / len(df):.1f}\n")


# ── Step 3: Embed chunks ────────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 60)
print(f"STEP 3: Embedding {len(all_chunks)} chunks with {EMBEDDING_MODEL}")
print("=" * 60)

model = SentenceTransformer(EMBEDDING_MODEL)
texts = [c["text"] for c in all_chunks]
embeddings = model.encode(
    texts,
    batch_size=BATCH_SIZE,
    show_progress_bar=True,
    convert_to_numpy=True
)
print(f"\n  Embedding matrix shape: {embeddings.shape}\n")


# ── Step 4: Build FAISS index ───────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 60)
print("STEP 4: Building FAISS index")
print("=" * 60)

dim = embeddings.shape[1]
embeddings_norm = embeddings.copy()
faiss.normalize_L2(embeddings_norm)

index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
index.add(embeddings_norm)
print(f"  Index built: {index.ntotal} vectors, {dim} dimensions\n")


# ── Step 5: Save artifacts ──────────────────────────────────────────────
print("=" * 60)
print("STEP 5: Saving artifacts")
print("=" * 60)

np.save(OUTPUT_DIR / "embeddings.npy", embeddings_norm)
faiss.write_index(index, str(OUTPUT_DIR / "faiss_index.bin"))
with open(OUTPUT_DIR / "chunks_metadata.json", "w", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    json.dump(all_chunks, f, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2)

index_size_mb = (OUTPUT_DIR / "faiss_index.bin").stat().st_size / 1_000_000
emb_size_mb   = (OUTPUT_DIR / "embeddings.npy").stat().st_size / 1_000_000
meta_size_mb  = (OUTPUT_DIR / "chunks_metadata.json").stat().st_size / 1_000_000

print(f"  faiss_index.bin      : {index_size_mb:.1f} MB")
print(f"  embeddings.npy       : {emb_size_mb:.1f} MB")
print(f"  chunks_metadata.json : {meta_size_mb:.1f} MB")
print(f"\n  Total artifacts: {index_size_mb + emb_size_mb + meta_size_mb:.1f} MB")
print("\nKnowledge base build complete.")
```

---

# Step 7 — `src/llm.py` Module

The core runtime module loaded by the Streamlit app. All LLM logic lives here.

```{python}
"""
src/llm.py
==========
Runtime LLM and RAG module for the CRPD Dashboard.
Loaded by app.py; used by tab_overview, tab_analyze, tab_explore.
"""

import json
import numpy as np
import faiss
import streamlit as st
from groq import Groq
import ollama
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer


# ── Constants ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
EMBEDDING_MODEL = "all-mpnet-base-v2"
FAISS_INDEX_PATH = "data/faiss_index.bin"
CHUNKS_PATH = "data/chunks_metadata.json"

# Model routing
MODEL_INSIGHT  = "llama3"                       # Ollama local — free for auto-summaries
MODEL_CHAT     = "llama-3.3-70b-versatile"      # Groq for Q&A
MODEL_REPORT   = "llama-3.3-70b-versatile"      # Groq for reports

CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT = """
You are an expert research analyst for the Institute on Disability and Public Policy (IDPP)
at American University. You assist researchers, policymakers, and disability rights advocates
in analyzing CRPD (UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities) reporting data.

Rules you must follow:
- Answer ONLY using the data context and document excerpts provided to you.
- Never invent statistics, country names, or document references.
- When citing specific findings, state the country, year, and document type.
- If the provided context does not contain enough information to answer, say so explicitly.
- Use formal, accessible language appropriate for policy and academic audiences.
- Frame answers through a disability rights lens — the shift from medical to rights-based
  models of disability is a central theme of this research.
- Keep answers concise unless the user requests detail.
"""


# ── Cached resource loaders ──────────────────────────────────────────────
@st.cache_resource
def get_groq_client():
    return Groq(api_key=st.secrets["GROQ_API_KEY"])


@st.cache_resource
def load_embedding_model() -> SentenceTransformer:
    return SentenceTransformer(EMBEDDING_MODEL)


@st.cache_resource
def load_search_index():
    index = faiss.read_index(FAISS_INDEX_PATH)
    with open(CHUNKS_PATH, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        chunks = json.load(f)
    return index, chunks


# ── Context builders ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def build_data_context(df, filters_summary: str = "") -> str:
    """Summarize the current filtered DataFrame for inclusion in prompts."""
    if df is None or len(df) == 0:
        return "No documents match the current filters."

    total = len(df)
    countries = df["country"].nunique()
    regions = df["region"].nunique()
    year_range = (f"{int(df['year'].min())}–{int(df['year'].max())}"
                  if "year" in df.columns else "N/A")
    doc_type_counts = df["doc_type"].value_counts().to_dict()

    return f"""
CURRENT DASHBOARD STATE:
- {total} documents from {countries} countries across {regions} regions ({year_range})
- Document types: {doc_type_counts}
- Active filters: {filters_summary if filters_summary else 'None'}
"""


# ── Semantic search ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def semantic_search(query: str, top_k: int = 8,
                    filter_country: str = None,
                    filter_doc_type: str = None,
                    filter_year_min: int = None,
                    filter_year_max: int = None) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Embed a query and retrieve top_k most relevant document chunks.
    Optional metadata filters applied post-retrieval.
    """
    model = load_embedding_model()
    index, chunks = load_search_index()

    query_vec = model.encode([query], convert_to_numpy=True)
    faiss.normalize_L2(query_vec)

    # Retrieve more than top_k to allow for post-filtering
    k_retrieve = min(top_k * 4, len(chunks))
    scores, indices = index.search(query_vec, k_retrieve)

    results = []
    for score, idx in zip(scores[0], indices[0]):
        if idx < 0 or idx >= len(chunks):
            continue
        chunk = chunks[idx]

        # Apply metadata filters
        if filter_country and chunk.get("country") != filter_country:
            continue
        if filter_doc_type and chunk.get("doc_type") != filter_doc_type:
            continue
        if filter_year_min and chunk.get("year") and chunk["year"] < filter_year_min:
            continue
        if filter_year_max and chunk.get("year") and chunk["year"] > filter_year_max:
            continue

        results.append({**chunk, "score": float(score)})
        if len(results) >= top_k:
            break

    return results


def format_retrieved_chunks(chunks: list[dict]) -> str:
    """Format retrieved chunks into a readable context block for prompts."""
    parts = []
    for i, c in enumerate(chunks, 1):
        header = f"[Source {i}: {c['country']}, {c['year']}, {c['doc_type'].title()}]"
        parts.append(f"{header}\n{c['text']}")
    return "\n\n---\n\n".join(parts)


# ── LLM call functions ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def ask_llm(user_message: str, context: str = "",
            model: str = MODEL_CHAT, max_tokens: int = 1024) -> str:
    """Single-turn LLM call. Routes to Ollama (local) or Groq."""
    full_message = f"{context}\n\n{user_message}" if context else user_message
    prompt_with_system = f"{CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT}\n\n{full_message}"

    if model == "llama3":
        # Ollama local inference (free)
        response = ollama.chat(model="llama3", messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT},
            {"role": "user", "content": full_message}
        ])
        return response["message"]["content"]
    else:
        # Groq API
        client = get_groq_client()
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=[
                {"role": "system", "content": CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT},
                {"role": "user", "content": full_message}
            ],
            max_tokens=max_tokens
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.content


def ask_llm_multiturn(messages: list[dict], context: str = "",
                      model: str = MODEL_CHAT, max_tokens: int = 1500) -> str:
    """Multi-turn chat call. `messages` is a list of {role, content} dicts."""
    # Inject data context into the first user message
    augmented = messages.copy()
    if context and augmented and augmented[0]["role"] == "user":
        augmented[0] = {
            "role": "user",
            "content": f"{context}\n\n{augmented[0]['content']}"
        }

    if model == "llama3":
        # Ollama local inference (free)
        ollama_msgs = [{"role": "system", "content": CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT}] + augmented
        response = ollama.chat(model="llama3", messages=ollama_msgs)
        return response["message"]["content"]
    else:
        # Groq API
        client = get_groq_client()
        groq_msgs = [{"role": "system", "content": CRPD_SYSTEM_PROMPT}] + augmented
        response = client.chat.completions.create(
            model=model,
            messages=groq_msgs,
            max_tokens=max_tokens
        )
        return response.choices[0].message.content


def rag_answer(query: str, df, top_k: int = 8,
               model: str = MODEL_CHAT) -> tuple[str, list[dict]]:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: embed query → retrieve chunks → answer with LLM.
    Returns (answer_text, retrieved_chunks) for display with citations.
    """
    retrieved = semantic_search(query, top_k=top_k)
    evidence = format_retrieved_chunks(retrieved)
    data_ctx = build_data_context(df)

    prompt = f"""{data_ctx}

RELEVANT DOCUMENT EXCERPTS:
{evidence}

QUESTION: {query}

Answer based on the excerpts above. Cite specific sources by country, year,
and document type where relevant."""

    answer = ask_llm(prompt, model=model, max_tokens=1500)
    return answer, retrieved


def generate_report(df, article_presets: dict, scope_label: str,
                    model: str = MODEL_REPORT) -> str:
    """Generate a structured policy brief for the current filtered data."""
    from src.analysis import article_frequency, model_shift_table

    data_ctx = build_data_context(df, scope_label)

    art_freq = article_frequency(df, article_presets)
    top_articles = (art_freq.groupby("article")["count"].sum()
                    .nlargest(5).to_dict() if not art_freq.empty else {})

    mt = model_shift_table(df)
    rights_pct = (mt["rights"].sum() / (mt["rights"].sum() + mt["medical"].sum()) * 100
                  if len(mt) else 0)

    top_countries = (df.groupby("country").size().nlargest(5).to_dict()
                     if len(df) else {})

    prompt = f"""
{data_ctx}

ADDITIONAL STATISTICS:
- Top 5 CRPD articles by keyword mentions: {top_articles}
- Rights-based language share: {rights_pct:.1f}%
- Top 5 countries by document count: {top_countries}
- Scope: {scope_label}

Generate a structured policy brief with the following sections:

## Executive Summary
(2–3 sentences summarizing the key story in this data)

## Key Findings
(4–5 bullet points with specific statistics from the data above)

## Thematic Analysis
(2–3 paragraphs on the most notable patterns, framed for a policy audience)

## Regional and Temporal Observations
(What does the data show about geographic and time-based trends?)

## Recommendations for Further Analysis
(What follow-up questions does this data raise? What analyses would be valuable?)

Use formal policy language. Reference specific articles, countries, and years
where the data supports it. Do not invent figures not present in the context above.
"""
    return ask_llm(prompt, model=model, max_tokens=2000)
```

---

# Step 8 — Phased Development Plan

## Phase 1 — Foundation & AI Insights
**Timeline: Week 1 | Prerequisites: API key only**

- [ ] Add `groq` and `ollama` to `requirements.txt`
- [ ] Create `.streamlit/secrets.toml` with `GROQ_API_KEY`
- [ ] Create `src/llm.py` with `get_groq_client`, `build_data_context`, `ask_llm`
- [ ] Add "AI Insights" expander to `tab_overview.py`
  - Button triggers Ollama llama3 call summarizing current filtered data
  - Output: 3–4 bullet point narrative of what the current view shows
- [ ] Test with 5–10 sample filter states

**Deliverable:** Users can get an AI-generated plain-English summary of any filter state in the Overview tab.

---

## Phase 2 — Conversational Chat Interface
**Timeline: Week 2 | Prerequisites: Phase 1 | Status: ✅ Complete (2026-03-18)**

> **Architectural decision — standalone page:** The original design placed this chat interface
> as a radio option inside `tab_analyze.py`. The implementation intentionally departed from
> this: the AI Research Assistant is a **standalone top-level page** at `?page=chat`, routed
> via `app.py` → `tab_chat.render(df_all)`. It has its own navbar entry (`smart_toy` icon)
> and is not embedded in the Analysis tab. Rationale: full-width layout, cleaner UX, and
> consistent use of the complete unfiltered `df_all` dataset regardless of sidebar filter state.

- [x] Add `tab_chat.py` as standalone page at `?page=chat` (not embedded in `tab_analyze.py`)
- [x] Implement `st.chat_input` + `st.session_state.chat_history`
- [x] Use `ask_llm_multiturn` with live data context from full dataset (`df_all`)
- [x] Add "Clear conversation" button
- [x] Add example starter questions to the UI
- [x] Add top-level navbar entry with `smart_toy` Material Icon

**Deliverable:** Multi-turn chat interface as a standalone page. Users ask questions; Groq
answers using statistics from the complete unfiltered DataFrame. No RAG yet — answers are
based on aggregated data context only.

**Evaluation results (2026-03-19):** 13 questions (incl. 4 multi-turn turns), 0 errors.
Overall avg 4.92/5.0 (PASS). Rate limit guard: PASS. Out-of-scope refusal: PASS.
Multi-turn context retention: PASS.

---

## Phase 3 — Knowledge Base Build & RAG
**Timeline: Weeks 3–4 | Prerequisites: PDF files + Phase 2**

- [ ] Obtain 585 source PDF files; place in `data/pdfs/`
- [ ] Install `pymupdf4llm`, `sentence-transformers`, `faiss-cpu`
- [ ] Run `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py` (one-time, ~15–20 min)
- [ ] Commit `faiss_index.bin`, `embeddings.npy`, `chunks_metadata.json` to repo
- [ ] Add `semantic_search`, `rag_answer` to `src/llm.py`
- [ ] Upgrade the Phase 2 chat to use RAG: question → semantic search → Groq + evidence
- [ ] Display retrieved source citations below each answer
- [ ] Add semantic search bar to the Document Explorer sub-tab in `tab_explore.py`

**Deliverable:** Chat answers are now grounded in actual document text with citations. Users can also use semantic search to find relevant documents by meaning rather than exact keywords.

---

## Phase 4 — Report Generation
**Timeline: Week 5 | Prerequisites: Phase 3**

- [x] Add `generate_policy_brief` to `src/llm.py` (supersedes `generate_report`)
- [x] ~~Add "Generate Research Brief" button to Document Explorer in `tab_explore.py`~~ → **Approved change (2026-03-19):** Implemented as standalone `src/tab_brief.py` page (`?page=brief`) with dedicated left/right panel layout, region popover (Geographic + Organization), multi-country RAG scope, and auto-populated sources from chunk metadata. Approved at design stage.
- [x] ~~Stream the report output for better UX~~ → **Approved change (2026-03-19):** Spinner used instead — acceptable for brief-length output.
- [x] ~~Add download button for Markdown and plain text export~~ → **Approved change (2026-03-19):** Export removed — reserved for paid subscribers (subscription feature).
- [ ] Add "AI Summary" button to Country Profiles sub-tab in `tab_explore.py` *(deferred — out of scope for Phase 4 revised design)*

**Deliverable:** Dedicated Policy Brief Generator page — region/country/article/date scoping → Groq llama-3.3-70b → structured 4-section brief with generation stats.

---

## Phase 5 — Evaluation & Quality Assurance
**Timeline: Ongoing from Phase 3 | Prerequisites: Phase 3**

Systematic measurement of retrieval quality and answer accuracy to guide iteration.

### Build CRPD Test Set

- [ ] Create a JSONL file of 50+ human-written question–answer pairs across categories:
  - **Single-article lookup** — e.g., "What does Article 24 say about inclusive education?"
  - **Cross-country comparison** — e.g., "How do Rwanda and Kenya differ on Article 9 accessibility?"
  - **Temporal trends** — e.g., "How has reporting on Article 19 changed between 2010 and 2025?"
  - **Rights vs medical framing** — e.g., "Which countries still use predominantly medical-model language?"
  - **Multi-document synthesis** — e.g., "What are common gaps across all State Party initial reports?"
  - **Numerical/statistical** — e.g., "How many countries have submitted more than one report?"
- [ ] Include expected source documents (country, year, type) for each question
- [ ] Tag each question by difficulty (easy, medium, hard) and category

### Retrieval Evaluation

- [ ] Measure **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)** — how quickly the correct chunk appears in results (target: > 0.7)
- [ ] Measure **nDCG** (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain) — overall ranking quality (target: > 0.8)
- [ ] Measure **Keyword Coverage** — do retrieved chunks contain expected key terms? (target: > 0.6)
- [ ] Log retrieval results per test question for before/after comparison when tuning

### Answer Quality Evaluation (LLM-as-Judge)

- [ ] Use a separate Groq call to score each answer 1–5 on:
  - **Accuracy** — Is the answer factually correct given the source documents?
  - **Completeness** — Does it cover all aspects of the question?
  - **Relevance** — Does it directly address what was asked?
  - **Groundedness** — Is every claim supported by a retrieved chunk? (hallucination detection)
- [ ] Flag any answer where a cited article, country, or year does not appear in the retrieved sources

### Human Evaluation Baseline

- [ ] Have 2–3 team members independently answer 20 test questions using only the source documents
- [ ] Use human answers as ground truth to calibrate the LLM-as-judge scores
- [ ] Identify question categories where human and LLM-judge scores diverge

### Advanced Retrieval Techniques (conditional on evaluation results)

- [ ] **Query rewriting** — Use Ollama llama3 to reformulate vague user questions before retrieval
- [ ] **Reranking** — Use Ollama llama3 to reorder top-k retrieved chunks by true relevance before generation
- [ ] **Multi-pass retrieval** — Run query variants and merge/deduplicate results for higher recall
- [ ] Only implement each technique if evaluation metrics show room for improvement

### Ongoing Monitoring

- [ ] Log all production queries, retrieved chunks, and answers for periodic review
- [ ] Track answer quality metrics weekly during initial rollout
- [ ] Set up alerts for retrieval failures (zero results or very low similarity scores)

**Deliverable:** A reproducible evaluation pipeline that measures retrieval and answer quality, with a human-calibrated baseline and clear improvement targets.

---

## Phase Summary

| Phase | Feature | Timeline | LLM | Cost/Query |
|-------|---------|----------|-----|-----------|
| 1 | AI Insights panel | Week 1 | Ollama llama3 | Free (local) |
| 2 | Chat Q&A (data context) | Week 2 | Groq | Free |
| 3 | RAG chat + semantic search | Weeks 3–4 | Groq | Free |
| 4 | Report generation | Week 5 | Groq | Free |
| 5 | Evaluation & quality assurance | Ongoing | Ollama llama3 (judge) | Free (local) |

---

## Development Workflow Per Phase

Each phase follows a structured pipeline rather than jumping straight to code:

| Step | Activity | Output |
|:----:|----------|--------|
| 1 | **Requirements** | Problem definition and acceptance criteria (plan documents) |
| 2 | **Design** | UI mockups in Pencil for all user-facing components |
| 3 | **Infrastructure** | Backend logic — embeddings, FAISS, API calls, data pipelines |
| 4 | **Integration** | Wire backend into Streamlit using the design as spec |
| 5 | **Evaluate** | Run Phase 5 metrics against test set; iterate if needed |

- [ ] Design Phase 1 UI (AI Insights panel) in Pencil before coding
- [ ] Design Phase 2 UI (Chat Q&A interface) in Pencil before coding
- [ ] Design Phase 3 UI (semantic search bar, source citations) in Pencil before coding
- [ ] Design Phase 4 UI (report generation button, download panel) in Pencil before coding

Designing the UI before generating code ensures consistency with the existing dashboard (UN Blue theme, WCAG 2.2, Plotly charts) and reduces iteration cycles.

---

# Cost Management

## Strategies

| Strategy | Implementation |
|----------|---------------|
| **Model routing** | Ollama llama3 for simple summaries (free); Groq for chat and reports (free tier) |
| **Response caching** | `@st.cache_data` on deterministic calls (same context → same answer) |
| **Chunk truncation** | Cap each retrieved chunk at 600 words before including in prompt |
| **Top-k limit** | Default to top 6–8 chunks; never more than 10 |
| **Embeddings are free** | `sentence-transformers` runs locally — zero API cost |
| **Session rate limiting** | Track calls per session in `st.session_state`; soft-cap at 20/session |
| **Context window discipline** | Never pass the full DataFrame to the LLM; always use `build_data_context` summary |

## Estimated Monthly Cost (Low Usage Research Tool)

Assuming ~50 active users/month, 10 queries/user, split across phases:

| Operation | Volume | Est. Cost |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| AI Insights (Ollama llama3) | 500 calls | Free (local) |
| Chat Q&A (Groq) | 300 calls | Free (Groq free tier) |
| RAG Q&A (Groq) | 200 calls | Free (Groq free tier) |
| Report Generation (Groq) | 50 calls | Free (Groq free tier) |
| **Total** | | **$0/month** |

---

# New Dependencies Summary

Add to `requirements.txt`:

```
# LLM & RAG
groq>=0.13.0
ollama>=0.4.0
sentence-transformers>=3.0.0
faiss-cpu>=1.8.0
pymupdf4llm>=0.0.17
```

---

# Security & Privacy Considerations

| Concern | Mitigation |
|---------|-----------|
| API key exposure | Store in `.streamlit/secrets.toml`; never hardcode; git-ignore the file |
| Sending PII to LLM | CRPD documents are public UN records — no PII concern |
| Prompt injection | System prompt rules are firm; user input is never interpolated into system prompt |
| Cost runaway | Session-level call counter + Streamlit caching on deterministic calls |
| Data in transit | All Groq API calls are HTTPS; Ollama runs locally with no data leaving the machine |

---

# Implementation Status (as of March 2026)

This section tracks the current state of each component against the plan.

## Infrastructure Status

| Component | Status | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| `crpd_reports.csv` | ✅ Complete | 585 documents, 155 countries, 2010–2026 |
| `data/pdfs/` | 🟡 Partial | 27 of 585 PDFs downloaded |
| `data/markdown/` | ❌ Not started | Requires PDFs first |
| `data/embeddings.npy` | ❌ Not started | Requires markdown conversion first |
| `data/faiss_index.bin` | ❌ Not started | Requires embeddings first |
| `data/chunks_metadata.json` | ❌ Not started | Requires chunking first |
| `.streamlit/secrets.toml` | ❌ Not created | API key needed |
| `src/llm.py` | ✅ Complete | `get_client`, `build_data_context`, `ask_llm`, `ask_llm_multiturn`, rate limit guard |

## Script Status

| Script | Status | Notes |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `LLM_Development/download_pdfs.py` | ❌ Not created | Planned; symbol-to-URL logic defined |
| `LLM_Development/sync_new_documents.py` | ❌ Not created | Planned; UN Digital Library API endpoint identified |
| `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py` | ❌ Not created | Full code outlined in this document |
| `.github/workflows/sync_crpd.yml` | ✅ Exists | Monthly sync workflow defined |

## Phase Status

| Phase | Feature | Status | Notes |
|-------|---------|--------|-------|
| 1 | AI Insights panel | ✅ Complete (2026-03-18) | `src/llm.py` + `tab_overview.py`; eval avg 3.58/5.0 |
| 2 | Chat Q&A interface | ✅ Complete (2026-03-18) | Standalone `tab_chat.py` at `?page=chat`; eval avg 4.92/5.0 |
| 3 | RAG + semantic search | ❌ Not started | Depends on PDF collection + Phase 2 |
| 4 | Policy brief generation | ❌ Not started | Depends on Phase 3 |
| 5 | Evaluation & quality assurance | ❌ Not started | Depends on Phase 3 |

## Dashboard Features Already Completed

The following dashboard improvements have been implemented and are relevant to the LLM integration:

- **Dynamic smart Key Insights** — `generate_smart_insights()` in `src/analysis.py` reads from visible chart data and updates with filters. This pattern will extend naturally to AI-powered insights in Phase 1.
- **100% stacked area chart** — Rights vs Medical model shift visualization on the homepage, providing the visual context for model shift analysis.
- **Bar chart for document submissions** — Documents per year with inline labels.
- **Bump chart with collision avoidance** — Top 10 article rank trajectories over time.
- **WCAG 2.2 compliance** — All font sizes meet the 14px minimum floor; visible focus outlines; keyboard navigation.
- **Global Plotly theme** — Consistent chart styling with UN Blue color scheme.

## Next Steps

Phases 1 and 2 are complete. The next milestone is Phase 3 (RAG + Semantic Search).

1. **Design Phase 3 UI** — Create Pencil mockup for semantic search bar and source citations in `LLM_Development/designs/`
2. **Complete PDF collection** — Run `LLM_Development/download_pdfs.py` to download remaining PDFs (27 of 585 downloaded)
3. **Build knowledge base** — Run `LLM_Development/build_knowledge_base.py` once PDFs are ready (PDF → Markdown → chunks → FAISS)
4. **Infrastructure** — Add `semantic_search` and `rag_answer` to `src/llm.py`
5. **Integration** — Upgrade `tab_chat.py` chat to use RAG; add semantic search to Document Explorer

---

# Appendix A — Decision Summary

| Decision | Choice | Reason |
|----------|--------|--------|
| PDF converter | `pymupdf4llm` | Best speed/quality ratio for digital PDFs |
| Fallback converter | `docling` | Best quality if pymupdf4llm output is insufficient |
| Chunking strategy | Semantic (by section) | Produces coherent, self-contained chunks |
| Chunk size | 500 words / 75 overlap | Fits embedding model limits; enough context per chunk |
| Embedding model | `all-mpnet-base-v2` | Best English quality; free; local |
| Vector store | FAISS (`IndexFlatIP`) | Zero infrastructure; fast enough at 15K chunks |
| LLM | Ollama llama3 (local) + Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile (Groq API) | Free local inference for summaries; free-tier API for chat and reports |
| Model routing | Ollama for summaries; Groq for chat and reports | Cost optimization by task complexity; entire stack is $0/month |
| Deployment | Posit Cloud (existing) | No infrastructure changes needed |

---

# Appendix B — Glossary

**RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)**
: A pattern where relevant documents are retrieved from a knowledge base and included in
  the LLM's prompt, grounding its answers in real source material rather than training
  knowledge.

**Embedding**
: A dense numerical vector representing the semantic meaning of a piece of text. Two
  semantically similar texts will have embeddings that are geometrically close.

**Chunk**
: A fixed-size or semantically-bounded segment of a document, used as the unit of
  indexing and retrieval in the RAG pipeline.

**FAISS**
: Facebook AI Similarity Search. An open-source library for efficient nearest-neighbor
  search over dense vectors. Used as the vector store in this integration.

**Cosine Similarity**
: A measure of similarity between two vectors based on the angle between them.
  Used to rank retrieved chunks by relevance to a query.

**Context Window**
: The maximum number of tokens an LLM can process in a single call. Groq llama-3.3-70b-versatile's
  context window is 128,000 tokens — far larger than needed for this application.

**Sentence Transformers**
: A Python library providing pre-trained models for generating sentence and text
  embeddings. All models used in this plan are free and run locally.
